problems.title: Number Theory - n^7 - n is divisible by 42
problems.problem: Show that $n^7 - n$ is divisible by $42$ for every positive integer $n$.
problems.hint:
• A number is divisible by $42$ if it is divisible by $6$ and $7$.
  • Use Fermat's Little Theorem to show that it is divisible by $7$.
problems.solution:
Note that a number is divisible by $42$ if it is divisible by $7$ and $6$, that is, divisible by $7,2$ and $3$. Now we have \begin{align*} n^7 - n & = n(n^6 - 1) = n ((n^2)^3 - 1) \\ & = n (n^2 - 1)(n^4 + n^2 + 1) \\ & = n (n-1)(n+1) (n^4 + n^2 + 1). \end{align*} Since the product any three consecutive integers is divisible by $6$, so $n^7 - n$ is divisible by $6$.
To show that it is divisible by $7$, we will use Fermat's Little Theorem. By Fermat's Little Theorem, \[ n^7 \equiv n \ (\mathrm{mod}\ 7) \implies 7 \mid (n^7 - n). \] Therefore, the number $n^7 - n$ is divisible by $42$.

problems.title: Probability - The function fx) = 6x (1 - x) is a probability density function
problems.problem:
The diameter of an electric cable, say $X$ is assumed to be a continuous random variable with probability density function (pdf)
    \[
        f(x) = 6x (1 - x), \quad 0 \leq x\leq 1.
    \]
    Check that above is a pdf and determine a number $b$ such that $P(X < b) = P(X > b)$.
problems.hint:
• $f$ is pdf if $\int_{-\infty }^{\infty } f(x) \mathrm{d} x = 1$.
  • $P(X< b) = \int_0^b f(x) \mathrm{d} x$ and $P(x > b) = \int_b^1 f(x) \mathrm{d} x$.
problems.solution:
To see if the given function is a pdf or not, we will check if the total probability is $1$. That is, \begin{align*} \int_{-\infty }^ \infty f(x) \mathrm{d} x = 1. \end{align*} Note that, \begin{align*} \int_{-\infty }^ \infty f(x) \mathrm{d} x & \int_0^1 f(x) \mathrm{d} x \\ & = 6\int_0^1 x(1-x) \mathrm{d} x \\ & = 6 \int _0^1 (x - x^2) \mathrm{d} x \\ & = 6 \left[ \frac{x^2}{2} - \frac{x^3}{3} \right]_0^1 \\ & = 1. \end{align*}
Given that $P(X < b) = P(X > b)$, we have \begin{align*} & \int_0^b f(x) \mathrm{d} x = \int_b^1 f(x) \mathrm{d} x \\ \implies & 6 \int_0^b (x(1-x)) = 6 \int_1^b (x(1-x)) \\ \implies & \left[ \frac{x^2}{2} - \frac{x^3}{3} \right]_0^b = \left[ \frac{x^2}{2} - \frac{x^3}{3} \right]_b^1 \\ \implies & \left( \frac{b^2}{2} - \frac{b^3}{3} \right) = \left[ \left( \frac{1}{2} - \frac{1}{3} \right) - \left( \frac{b^2}{2} - \frac{b^3}{3} \right) \right] \\ \implies & 3b^2 - 2b^3 = 1 - 3b^2 + 2b^3 \\ \implies & 4b^3 - 6b^2 + 1 = 0 \\ \implies & (2b -1)(2b^2 - 2b - 1) = 0 \\ \implies & 2b - 1 = 0, \quad \text{ since } 2b^2 - 2b - 1 >0. \end{align*} Thus $b = \frac{1}{2}$.

problems.title: NBHM - Find the value of a in x^3 - 5x^2 + ax - 1
problems.problem:
Find the value of $a \in \mathbb{Z} $ such that $2 + \sqrt{3} $ is a root of the polynomial 
    \[
        p(x) = x^3 - 5x^2 + ax - 1.
    \]
problems.hint:
• The irrational roots are in conjugate pairs.
  • Apply ratioanl root theorem.
problems.solution: By ratioanl root theorem, the only possible rational roots are $\pm 1$. Also, since the polynomial is with integer coefficients, any irrational root will be in pair (with its conjugate). Therefore the possible roots are \[ \{ 2 + \sqrt{3} , 2 - \sqrt{3} , 1 \} \quad \text{ or } \{ 2 + \sqrt{3} , 2 - \sqrt{3} , -1 \}. \] Recall that the sum of roots of a polynomial \[ a_n x^n + a_{n-1} x^{n-1} + \dots + a_1 x + a_0, \] is $-\frac{a_{n-1} }{a_n}$. So in our case it is $5$. Therefore the roots are $\{ 2 + \sqrt{3} , 2 - \sqrt{3} , 1 \}.$ Therefore, \begin{align*} a & = (2 + \sqrt{3} ) (2 - \sqrt{3} ) + (2 + \sqrt{3} )(1) + (2-\sqrt{3} )(1) \\ & = (4 -3) + 2 + \sqrt{3} + 2 - \sqrt{3} \\ & = 5. \end{align*} Thus, $a = 5$.

problems.title: Calculus - NBHM - 2005 - div(curl(v)) = 0
problems.problem:
Let $\mathbf{v} = (v_1, v_2. v_3)$ be a vector field on $\mathbb{R} ^3$ where
    \[
        v_1 = \sqrt{1 + x^2 + y^2} , v_2 = \sqrt{1+ z^2} \text{ and } v_3 = \sqrt{1+ x^2 y^2 z^2}.
    \]
    Evaluate $\mathrm{div} (\mathrm{curl}(\mathbf{v} ) )$.
problems.hint: • Recall that if the components of a vector field have continuous second-order partial derivatives, then $\mathrm{div}(\mathrm{curl}(\mathbf{v})) = 0$.
problems.solution:
The curl of a vector field $\mathbf{v} = (v_1,v_2,v_3)$ is given by \[ \nabla \times \mathbf{v} = \left( \frac{\partial v_3}{\partial y} - \frac{\partial v_2}{\partial z}, \frac{\partial v_1}{\partial z} - \frac{\partial v_3}{\partial x}, \frac{\partial v_2}{\partial x} - \frac{\partial v_1}{\partial y} \right). \]
Taking the divergence, we obtain \begin{align*} \nabla \cdot (\nabla \times \mathbf{v}) &= \frac{\partial}{\partial x} \left( \frac{\partial v_3}{\partial y} - \frac{\partial v_2}{\partial z} \right) + \frac{\partial}{\partial y} \left( \frac{\partial v_1}{\partial z} - \frac{\partial v_3}{\partial x} \right) + \frac{\partial}{\partial z} \left( \frac{\partial v_2}{\partial x} - \frac{\partial v_1}{\partial y} \right) \\[0.5em] &= \frac{\partial^2 v_3}{\partial x \partial y} - \frac{\partial^2 v_2}{\partial x \partial z} + \frac{\partial^2 v_1}{\partial y \partial z} - \frac{\partial^2 v_3}{\partial y \partial x} + \frac{\partial^2 v_2}{\partial z \partial x} - \frac{\partial^2 v_1}{\partial z \partial y}. \end{align*} Since the functions $v_1, v_2,$ and $v_3$ have continuous second-order partial derivatives on $\mathbb{R}^3$, the mixed partial derivatives commute: \[ \frac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x \partial y} = \frac{\partial^2 f}{\partial y \partial x}. \] Hence, each pair of terms cancels, and we conclude that \[ \nabla \cdot (\nabla \times \mathbf{v}) = 0. \] Therefore, \[ \boxed{\mathrm{div}(\mathrm{curl}(\mathbf{v})) = 0.} \]

problems.title: NBHM 2005 - Find the area of the triangle
problems.problem: What is the area of the triangle in the complex plane formed by the points representing $1, \omega $ and $\omega ^2$, where $\omega $ is a complex cube root of unity?
problems.hint: • Draw picture and note that it is an equilateral triangle.
problems.solution:
We need to find the area of the triangle formed by the points representing $1, \omega $ and $\omega ^2$. The triangle is equilateral triangle and the side length is \begin{align*} \left\vert 1 - \omega \right\vert^2 = \left\vert \left( \frac{3}{2} \right)^2 + \left( \frac{-\sqrt{3} }{2} \right)^2 \right\vert = 3. \end{align*}
Thus, the side length is $\sqrt{3} $. Therefore, the area of the triangle will be \begin{align*} A & = \frac{\sqrt{3} }{4} (\text{ side } ^2) = \frac{\sqrt{3} }{4} (\sqrt{3} ^2) = \frac{3\sqrt{3}}{4}. \end{align*}

problems.title: Differential Geometry - On R x^3 is different chart than the identity one
problems.problem:
Prove that the function 
    \[
        \phi : \mathbb{R} \to  \mathbb{R} ,  \quad  \varphi (s) = s^3,
    \]
    defines a $C^{\infty} $ differentiable structure on $\mathbb{R} $ different from the usual one (that of the atlas $\{ (\mathbb{R} , \mathrm{Id}_{\mathbb{R} } ) \} $ ).
problems.hint:
• Consider the single chart \((\mathbb{R}, \varphi)\) with \(\varphi(s) = s^3\). Show that this chart defines a smooth atlas on \(\mathbb{R}\).
  • To prove that this smooth structure is different from the standard one, compare it with the usual chart \((\mathbb{R}, \mathrm{Id}_{\mathbb{R}})\). Study the transition map \[ \mathrm{Id}_{\mathbb{R}} \circ \varphi^{-1}(t) = t^{1/3} \] and check whether it is \(C^\infty\) at \(t = 0\).
problems.solution:
First of all we will show that $(\mathbb{R} ,\varphi) $ is an atlas for $\mathbb{R} $. Since $\varphi ^{-1} (s) = \sqrt[3]{s} $, so $\varphi $ is a homomorphism and hence $(\mathbb{R} , \varphi )$ is an atlas for $\mathbb{R} $.
Recall that a differentiable structure on a topological manifold $M$ is given by a maximal smooth atlas. Two differentiable structures on the same topological manifold $M$ are said to be the same if their maximal atlases coincide. Equivalently, two atlases define the same differentiable structure if and only if every chart in one atlas is smoothly compatible with every chart in the other. That is, their union is also a differentiable atlas.
Now to show that $(\mathbb{R} ,\varphi )$ is different than $(\mathbb{R} , \mathrm{Id}_{\mathbb{R} })$, we must check smooth compatibility between the charts $(\mathbb{R} , \mathrm{Id}_{\mathbb{R} } )$ and $(\mathbb{R} , \varphi )$, that is, $\{ (\mathbb{R} ,\mathrm{Id}_{\mathbb{R} } ), (\mathbb{R} , \varphi ) \} $ is not a differentiable atlas. The transition maps are \begin{align*} & \varphi \circ \mathrm{Id}^{-1} (s) = \varphi (s) = s^3, \text{ is } C^{\infty} \\ & \mathrm{Id}_{\mathbb{R} } \circ \varphi ^{-1} (s) \mathrm{Id}_{\mathbb{R} }(\sqrt[3]{s} ) = \sqrt[3]{s}, \text{ is not } C^{\infty} . \end{align*} Therefore, the two differentiable structures are different.

problems.title: Number Theory - 7^2n - 1 is divisible by 48
problems.problem: Prove that for every integer $n \geq 1$, the number $7^{2n} - 1$ is divisible by $48$.
problems.hint:
• A number is divisible by $48$ if and only if it is divisible by $16$ and $3$.
  • Show that $7^2 \equiv 1 \pmod{16}$ and that $7 \equiv 1 \pmod{3}$.
problems.solution: We need to show that for any natural number $n$, the number $7^{2n} - 1 $ is divisible by $48$. A number is divisible by $48$ if and only if it is divisible by $16$ and $3$ (as they are coprime). Note that \begin{align*} 7^2 = 49 \equiv 1 \ (\mathrm{mod}\ 16) & \implies 7^{2n} \equiv 1 \ (\mathrm{mod}\ 16)\\ & \implies \forall\ n \in \mathbb{N}, 7^{2n} - 1 \text{ is divisible by } 16. \\ 7 \equiv 1 \ (\mathrm{mod}\ 3) & \implies 7^{2n} \equiv 1 \ (\mathrm{mod}\ 3) \\ & \implies \forall\ n \in \mathbb{N}, 7^{2n} - 1 \text{ is divisible by } 3. \end{align*} Thus, the number $7^{2n} -1$ is divisible by $48$.

problems.title: Number Theory - sqrt{p} is irrational number
problems.problem: Let $p$ be a prime number. Show that $\sqrt{p} $ is an irrational number.
problems.hint: • Write $\sqrt{p} = \frac{m}{n}$ by assuming it is a rational number. Also, assume that $\gcd (m,n) = 1$. This implies $p$ divides $m^2$ and hence $m$.
problems.solution:
We need to show that for a prime number $p$, the number $\sqrt{p} $ is an irrational number. To the contrary, assume that $\sqrt{p} $ is a rational number. Write, \[ \sqrt{p} = \frac{m}{n}, \quad m,n \in \mathbb{Z} \text{ and } n\neq 0. \] Further assume that $\gcd (m,n) = 1$.
Observe that \begin{align*} \sqrt{p} = \frac{m}{n} & \implies p = \frac{m^2}{n^2} \\ & \implies pn^2 = m^2 \\ & \implies p \mid m^2 \implies p \mid m \\ & \implies m = kp, \quad \text{ for some } k \in \mathbb{Z}. \\ & \implies p = \frac{k^2 p^2}{n^2} \implies pk^2 = n^2 \\ & \implies p \mid n^2 \implies p \mid n. \end{align*} This is a contradiction as $\gcd (m,n) = 1$. Thus, $\sqrt{p} $ is an irrational number.

problems.title: Combinatorics - In how many ways can 10 identical balls be distributed into 4 distinct boxes such that each box contains at least one ball
problems.problem: In how many ways can 10 identical balls be distributed into 4 distinct boxes such that each box contains at least one ball?
problems.hint: • Apply the stars and bars formula.
problems.solution:
Let the number of balls in each box be $x_1, x_2, x_3, x_4$. Then we must solve \[ x_1 + x_2 + x_3 + x_4 = 10 \] with the condition \[ x_i \ge 1 \quad \text{for } i = 1,2,3,4. \]
Since each variable must be at least 1, set \[ x_i = y_i + 1. \] Substituting into the equation gives \[ (y_1 + 1) + (y_2 + 1) + (y_3 + 1) + (y_4 + 1) = 10. \] Simplifying, \[ y_1 + y_2 + y_3 + y_4 + 4 = 10 \implies y_1 + y_2 + y_3 + y_4 = 6, \] where $y_i \ge 0.$
Apply the Stars and Bars formula The number of nonnegative integer solutions of \[ y_1 + y_2 + y_3 + y_4 = 6 \] is given by \[ \binom{n + k - 1}{k - 1}, \] where \( n = 6 \) and \( k = 4 \). Thus, \[ \binom{6 + 4 - 1}{4 - 1} = \binom{9}{3} = 84. \] Therefore, the number of ways to distribute the balls is \( 84 \).

problems.title: Probability - Given the probability density function, find the value of a
problems.problem:
Let $X$ be a continuous random variable with probability density function (PDF) \[ f(x) = \begin{cases} ax, &\text{ if } 0 \leq x \leq 1 ;\\ a, &\text{ if } 1 \leq x \leq 2; \\ -ax + 3a, & \text{ if } 2 \leq x\leq 3; \\ 0, & \text{ otherwise }. \end{cases} \]
  1. Determine the constant $a$.
  2. Compute $P(X \leq 1.5)$.
problems.hint: • Note that $f(x)$ is the PDF, so $\int_{-\infty }^{\infty } f(x) \mathrm{d} x = 1$. Use this to determine $a$.
problems.solution:
1. To determine the constant $a$, we need to use that $f$ is the PDF of the random variable $X$, that is, $\int_{-\infty }^{\infty } f(x) \mathrm{d} x = 1$. So, \begin{align*} & \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f(x) \,\mathrm{d}x = 1\\ \implies & \left( \int_{-\infty }^0 + \int_{0}^1 + \int_{1}^2 + \int_2^3 + \int_3^{\infty } \right) f(x) \mathrm{d} x = 1 \\ \implies & \int_{-\infty }^0 0\ \mathrm{d} x + \int_0^1 ax\ \mathrm{d} x + \int_1^2 a \ \mathrm{d} x \\ & \qquad + \int_2^3 (-ax + 3a) \mathrm{d} x + \int_3^{\infty } 0 \ \mathrm{d} x = 1 \\ \implies & \left. \frac{ax^2}{2} \right| _0^1 + ax \Big| _1^2 + \left[ -\frac{ax^2}{2} + 3ax \right]_2^3 = 1 \\ \implies & \frac{a}{2} + a + a \left[ \left( -\frac{9}{2}+9 \right) -(-2 + 6) \right] = 1 \\ \implies & \frac{a}{2} + a + \frac{a}{2} = 1 \\ \implies & a = \frac{1}{2}. \end{align*}
  2. Now we need to determine the probability $P(X \leq 1.5)$, that is, \begin{align*} P(X \leq 1.5) & = \int_{-\infty }^{1.5} f(x)\ \mathrm{d}x \\ & = \int_0^1 f(x)\ \mathrm{d} x + \int_1^{1.5} f(x) \ \mathrm{d} x \\ & = \int_0^1 \frac{x}{2}\ \mathrm{d} x + \int_1^{1.5} \frac{1}{2}\ \mathrm{d} x \\ & = \left[ \frac{x^2}{4} \right] _0^1 + \left[ \frac{x}{2} \right] _1^{1.5} \\ & = \frac{1}{4} + \frac{.5}{2} = \frac{1}{2}. \end{align*} Thus, \[ P(X \leq 1.5) = \frac{1}{2}. \]

problems.title: Number Theory - Every natural number with at least one odd prime divisor can be written as sum of consecuitive numbers
problems.problem: Show that any positive integer which has at least one odd prime factor can be written as a sum of two or more consecutive positive integers.
problems.hint: • Write $n$ as a product of its odd prime factor and the remaining part. Then write $n$ as a sum of $2k+1$ terms, where $2k+1$ is the odd factor. Finally, subtract $k, k-1, \dots, 1$ from the first $k$ terms and add $1, 2, \dots, k$ to the last $k$ terms.
problems.solution:
Let $n$ be a positive integer which has at least one odd prime factor. We will show that $n$ can be written as a sum of two or more consecutive positive integers.
Since $n$ has an odd prime factor, let $2k+1$ be an odd factor of $n$. Then we can write $n$ as \[ n = (2k+1) \cdot m, \quad \text{ for some } m \in \mathbb{N}. \] This is equivalent to \[ n = \underbrace{m + m + \dots + m}_{2k+1 \text{ times}}. \]
Now we will subtract $k, k-1, \dots, 1$ from the first $k$ terms and add $1, 2, \dots, k$ to the last $k$ terms. This gives us \begin{align*} n & = (m - k) + (m - (k - 1)) + \dots + (m-2) + (m - 1) + m \\ & \quad + (m + 1) + (m + 2) \dots + (m + (k - 1)) + (m + k) \\ & = (m - k) + (m - k + 1) + \dots + (m + k - 1) + (m + k). \end{align*} Thus, $n$ can be written as a sum of $2k+1$ consecutive positive integers.

problems.title: Number Theory - Square of any even integer is of the form $4k$ but not of the form $4k + 2
problems.problem: Show that square of any even integer is of the form $4k$ but not of the form $4k + 2$ for any integer $k$. Also, show that square of any odd integer is of the form $4k + 1$ for some integer $k$ but not of the form $4k + 3$ for any integer $k$.
problems.hint: • Write an even integer as $2k$ and an odd integer as $2k + 1$ for some integer $k$. Then compute the square of both and simplify.
problems.solution:
At first we will show that the square of any even integer is of the form $4k$ but not of the form $4k + 2$ for any integer $k$. Let $n$ be an even integer. Then we can write $n$ as \[ n = 2k, \quad \text{ for some } k \in \mathbb{Z}. \] Then we have \[ n^2 = (2k)^2 = 4k^2. \] Thus, the square of any even integer is of the form $4k$ for some integer $k$.
Now we will show that the square of any even integer is not of the form $4k + 2$ for any integer $k$. Assume that there exists an integer whose square is of the form $4k + 2$ for some integer $k$. Since $4k + 2$ is even, so the number must be even. Let us write that number as $2m$ for some integer $m$. Then we have \[ 4k + 2 = (2m)^2 = 4m^2 \implies 2 = 4m^2 - 4k = 4(m^2 - k). \] This implies that $2$ is divisible by $4$, which is a contradiction. Hence, the square of any even integer is not of the form $4k + 2$ for any integer $k$.
Now we will show that the square of any odd integer is of the form $4k + 1$ for some integer $k$. Let $n$ be an odd integer. Then we can write $n$ as \[ n = 2k + 1, \quad \text{ for some } k \in \mathbb{Z}. \] Then we have \[ n^2 = (2k + 1)^2 = 4k^2 + 4k + 1 = 4(k^2 + k) + 1. \] Thus, the square of any odd integer is of the form $4k + 1$ for some integer $k$.
Finally, we will show that the square of any odd integer is not of the form $4k + 3$ for any integer $k$. Assume that there exists an integer whose square is of the form $4k + 3$ for some integer $k$. Since $4k + 3$ is odd, so the number must be odd. Let us write that number as $2m + 1$ for some integer $m$. Then we have \begin{align*} 4k + 3 & = (2m + 1)^2 = 4m^2 + 4m + 1 = 4(m^2 + m) + 1 \\ \implies & 2 = 4(m^2 + m) - 4k = 4(m^2 + m - k). \end{align*} This implies that $2$ is divisible by $4$, which is a contradiction. Hence, the square of any odd integer is not of the form $4k + 3$ for any integer $k$.

problems.title: Probability -
problems.problem: A box contains 5 red balls and 7 green balls. Two balls are drawn at random without replacement. What is the probability that both balls are of the same color?
problems.hint: • Use the formula for combinations: $\binom{n}{r} = \frac{n!}{r!(n-r)!}$. Count the favorable outcomes (both balls same color) and divide by total outcomes.
problems.solution: We need to find the probability that both balls drawn are of the same color. The total number of ways to draw 2 balls from the box is \[ \binom{12}{2} = 66. \] The number of ways to draw 2 \textcolor{red}{red} balls is \[ \binom{5}{2} = 10, \] and the number of ways to draw 2 \textcolor{teal}{green} balls is \[ \binom{7}{2} = 21. \] Therefore, the total number of ways to draw 2 balls of the same color is \[ 10 + 21 = 31. \] Thus, the probability that both balls drawn are of the same color is \[ P(\text{both balls are of the same color}) = \frac{31}{66}. \]
